biological.physical_capacity: 5'8", lean functional build from three years of martial arts cross-training (muay thai, wrestling, jiu-jitsu). Moves economically — the kind of physical presence that registers with peers without being performed. Puberty accelerating through the summer.

attachment.forming_context: Karen is reliably present and warm, but her own anxiety transmits through her body language. Drew learns that love is dependable but slightly nervous.

active.name: emotional_inhibition
active.core_belief: Strong feelings make things worse — keep it together
active.compensatory_strategy: Channels emotion into productivity and problem-solving. Reads rooms before speaking. Defaults to 'I'm fine.' Marcus and Carol are partial exceptions — Drew is honest about thinking with them, though not about feeling. The schema is now being actively circumvented in Sofia's presence rather than managed around. The letter was still a structured medium; the courtyard conversation was live and unscripted. 'I want' came out before the filter could engage — desire moving faster than inhibition. With Carol, a structurally different event: Drew said the full 'I want' sentence not because desire outran the filter, but because the filter was simply absent. The schema did not engage. This is the first time Drew has expressed a naked relational desire fully, calmly, without override — and had it received without consequence.
active.forming_context: Greg models stoic competence. Karen's anxiety spikes when Drew is upset. Plano culture rewards cheerful compliance.
active.healing_note: Age 14 — the letter was the first act of expression over suppression. The courtyard conversation went further: live, unscripted, and 'I want' escaped unfiltered — but driven by desire overwhelming the inhibition reflex. The Carol conversation is the genuine healing event: Drew said the same sentence, in full, without desire overriding anything. The filter simply wasn't there. He expressed vulnerability in a calm, safe relational context and was received without consequence. This is the first experience of that. The schema is weakened, not resolved — it would still reassert under stress, with unfamiliar relational partners, or when the stakes feel higher. But Drew now has an internal model for what expression without suppression feels like. That's new.

active.name: self_sacrifice
active.core_belief: My job is to make things easier for the people around me
active.compensatory_strategy: Volunteers for tasks, anticipates what others need, measures his own worth by usefulness. Now extending to the friendship with Marcus — Drew sometimes defers to Marcus's project ideas without examining whether he wants something different. The schema has been quiet across two significant relational moments: the Sofia letter (written for no reason except want), and lunch in the courtyard (attended for no reason except want). The Marcus conversation is the interesting case — Drew proactively protected the friendship, but not from guilt or fear of loss. He did it because he wanted both things and saw no reason they couldn't coexist. The self-sacrifice pattern wasn't driving that; genuine care was.
active.forming_context: Post-9/11 household anxiety, Greg's job instability, Karen stretched thin. Drew decided to be the kid who doesn't add to the burden.

tested_by.context: Wrote a letter to Sofia Reyes — first act of self-direction in the relational/emotional domain. Not driven by competence or usefulness, but by want. Self-direction expanded from intellectual and physical domains into desire itself. Continued into reciprocal territory: showed up to the courtyard, stayed present in an unscripted conversation, said 'I want' out loud before the inhibition could catch it. Third consolidation: brought Sofia to Carol fully and without hedging — said the complete sentence in the one relationship where the filter doesn't operate. Self-direction is no longer confined to domains where Drew controls the outcome, and it now includes the act of naming what he wants to someone who matters.

values.last_reordered: age 13 — self-direction moved to top as Drew's competence domains (martial arts, computers, social navigation) gave him increasing confidence in his own judgment

self_concept.agency: strong and expanding — three years of martial arts competition, real software projects, and social fluidity across groups built a robust sense of capability. Now confirmed in a domain where competence doesn't apply: Drew cannot prepare for Sofia, cannot debug a conversation mid-stream, cannot anticipate what she'll say. He showed up anyway and was present rather than managing. Agency is no longer contingent on control.
self_concept.communion: selective but genuine — deep bonds with Marcus and Carol, broad-but-shallow connections across peer groups, comfort with being between rather than belonging to. Now in active reciprocal territory for the first time: Sofia approached him, not the other way around. She said 'same time tomorrow.' Drew is discovering that communion can be pursued rather than earned, and that protecting one bond (Marcus) doesn't require sacrificing another. Communion is not zero-sum. The Carol conversation adds a new layer: Drew now has the experience of full emotional disclosure — the unfiltered version — being received cleanly by someone he trusts. He knows what that feels like. This is a model he can carry into other relational contexts, including the moment with Sofia when it comes.
self_concept.dominant_sequence: emerging redemption arc — reactive excellence (ages 11-13) giving way to proactive intention. The realization that preparation without desire is just aimlessness. Three data points now: the letter, the 'I want' in the courtyard, and the full sentence to Carol. The beat has been laid three times. It is a pattern.
self_concept.identity_statement: I am someone who figures things out, connects across boundaries, and prepares for what's coming. I don't need to belong to one group to belong. I'm learning that wanting something — not because it's useful, not because someone else needs it, just because I want it — is something I can act on. The filter doesn't always win. And I'm learning something else: the gap between knowing what you want and feeling settled about it isn't a problem to fix. It's just most of what being a person feels like. You don't solve it. You get better at living inside it.

defenses.note: Anticipation added as a mature defense — Drew's preparation orientation (martial arts, systems thinking, social observation) is fundamentally about anticipating and preparing for challenges before they arrive. Defense profile is solidly mature under normal stress. Threshold unchanged. The Carol conversation involved no stress and required no defense mobilization — Drew was in his safest relational context. The open question remains: what happens when Sofia pulls back, or when the two-world problem creates real conflict rather than clean alignment. That's where the threshold will be tested.
